questions.question: Define force and explain its role in changing the motion of objects.
questions.answer: Force is a push or pull acting on an object. It can change the state of motion of an object by starting, stopping, speeding up or changing its direction.

questions.question: Explain the different effects of force with suitable examples.
questions.answer: Force can start motion, stop motion, change the speed of motion, change direction of motion or change the shape of an object.

questions.question: Differentiate between contact forces and non-contact forces with examples.
questions.answer: Contact forces require physical contact between objects, such as friction and muscular force. Non-contact forces act without physical contact, such as gravitational and magnetic forces.

questions.question: Explain muscular force and give examples from daily life.
questions.answer: Muscular force is the force exerted by muscles of humans or animals. Examples include lifting objects, pulling a cart and kicking a football.

questions.question: Explain friction and describe how it affects motion.
questions.answer: Friction is a force that opposes the motion of objects when they move over a surface. It slows down moving objects and can eventually stop them.

questions.question: Describe two advantages and two disadvantages of friction.
questions.answer: Friction helps us walk and allows vehicles to stop using brakes. However, it also causes wear and tear of machine parts and wastes energy as heat.

questions.question: Explain gravitational force and its importance.
questions.answer: Gravitational force is the force by which the Earth attracts objects towards its center. It keeps objects on the ground and causes things to fall.

questions.question: Explain why objects fall towards the Earth when dropped.
questions.answer: Objects fall towards the Earth because the Earth's gravitational force pulls them downward.

questions.question: Explain magnetic force and give examples.
questions.answer: Magnetic force is the force exerted by magnets that attract or repel magnetic materials such as iron. Magnets are used in devices like compasses.

questions.question: Describe how friction depends on the nature of surfaces.
questions.answer: Friction is greater on rough surfaces because they have more irregularities that oppose motion. Smooth surfaces produce less friction.

questions.question: Explain how force can change the direction of motion with an example.
questions.answer: Force applied to a moving object can change its direction. For example, hitting a moving ball with a bat changes its direction.

questions.question: Explain why friction is necessary for vehicles to move.
questions.answer: Friction between the tyres and the road allows vehicles to move forward without slipping.

questions.question: Explain why lubricants are used in machines.
questions.answer: Lubricants reduce friction between moving parts of machines and help them operate smoothly.

questions.question: Explain how friction produces heat.
questions.answer: When two surfaces rub against each other, friction converts some mechanical energy into heat energy.

questions.question: Explain why shoes have rough soles.
questions.answer: Shoes have rough soles to increase friction with the ground and prevent slipping.

questions.question: Explain how magnetic force can act without contact.
questions.answer: Magnetic force can attract or repel objects without touching them because magnetic fields act through space.

questions.question: Explain why astronauts appear weightless in space.
questions.answer: Astronauts appear weightless because they are in continuous free fall around the Earth while orbiting.

questions.question: Explain how force can change the speed of an object.
questions.answer: Applying force in the direction of motion can increase speed, while force applied opposite to motion can reduce speed.

questions.question: Explain the importance of studying forces in science.
questions.answer: Studying forces helps us understand motion, design machines and explain many natural phenomena.

questions.question: Explain how force acts in sports activities.
questions.answer: Forces act when players kick, throw, hit or catch balls during sports activities.

questions.question: Explain why heavy objects require more force to move.
questions.answer: Heavy objects have greater mass and require more force to overcome friction and start moving.

questions.question: Explain why magnets attract iron objects but not wooden objects.
questions.answer: Magnets attract iron because iron is a magnetic material, while wood does not respond to magnetic fields.

questions.question: Explain why friction is greater on rough surfaces.
questions.answer: Rough surfaces have more irregularities that interlock with each other, increasing friction.

questions.question: Explain how gravity helps keep planets in orbit.
questions.answer: Gravity provides the force that keeps planets moving in curved paths around the Sun.

questions.question: Explain how forces are important in everyday life.
questions.answer: Forces help us move objects, operate machines and perform daily activities such as walking, lifting and pushing.
